[Audio Version ($)](https://www.producttalk.org/give-claude-code-a-memory/#audio-version)

"Can you critique the landing page for my new [Story-Based Customer Interviews course](https://learn.producttalk.org/course/story-based-customer-interviews?ref=producttalk.org)?"

I used to waste hours trying to get [ChatGPT](https://chatgpt.com/?ref=producttalk.org) or [Claude](https://claude.ai/?ref=producttalk.org) to adequately critique my work. I'd get frustrated by the generic feedback, the poor writing, and the suggestions that just wouldn't work for my audience or my products.

But not anymore. Not only does Claude critique my work; it also helps me produce the work.

It helps me generate marketing copy—in my voice! It helps me write blog posts. It knows what search terms are relevant to my business and helps me optimize my articles for SEO and now AEO. It helps me with competitive research, academic research, and [discovery](https://www.producttalk.org/2021/08/product-discovery/) research. And it does all of this with little prompting from me.

![](https://storage.ghost.io/c/57/9b/579b6dca-f48a-4307-844f-f0533595d058/content/images/2026/04/Very-specific-SEO-advice-from-Claude-Code.webp)This isn't generic SEO advice. Claude understands my target audience. It knows I have a glossary SEO strategy, so it can help me balance competing demands of writing for a human audience while ranking well.

I don't have to upload files to a web-based project. I don't have to save and manage detailed prompts. I don't have to keep repeating myself. I just ask for help and Claude knows exactly what to do.

How did I get here?

I learned how to give [Claude Code](https://www.claude.com/product/claude-code?ref=producttalk.org) a memory. Claude knows who my target customer is, the key value propositions I focus on, the specific opportunities each product addresses, my revenue model, my marketing channels, and so much more.

So now when I ask Claude for help, it has all the right context it needs to be an expert helper. I get high-quality output tailored to my audience that works for my products and services. Every time.

And I don't have to keep giving Claude this context over and over again. Instead, it just remembers.

Today, I'm going to show you how I gave Claude a memory. It relies on Claude Code (which requires a Pro subscription), but I promise it will be worth it. If you are new to Claude Code, be sure to start with: [Claude Code: What It Is, How It's Different, and Why Non-Technical People Should Use It](https://producttalk.org/claude-code-what-it-is-and-how-its-different?ref=producttalk.org).

This article is the second in a series. Be sure to check out the other articles:

- [Claude Code: What It Is, How It's Different, and Why Non-Technical People Should Use It](https://www.producttalk.org/claude-code-what-it-is-and-how-its-different/)
- Stop Repeating Yourself: Give Claude Code a Memory
- [How to Use Claude Code Safely: A Non-Technical Guide to Managing Risk](https://www.producttalk.org/how-to-use-claude-code-safely/)
- [How to Choose Which Tasks to Automate with AI (+50 Real Examples)](https://www.producttalk.org/how-to-choose-which-tasks-to-automate-with-ai/)
- [How to Build AI Workflows with Claude Code (Even If You're Not Technical)](https://www.producttalk.org/how-to-build-ai-workflows-with-claude-code/)
- [How to Use Claude Code: A Guide to Slash Commands, Agents, Skills, and Plugins](https://www.producttalk.org/how-to-use-claude-code-features/)
- [Context Rot: Why AI Gets Worse the Longer You Chat (And How to Fix It)](https://www.producttalk.org/context-rot/)
- [How to Share Your AI Context and Skills Across Devices](https://www.producttalk.org/how-to-share-your-ai-context-and-skills/)

💡

This series was inspired by my personal usage of Claude Code. I'm a big fan and I like to share what works for me with readers.

I have not received any compensation from Anthropic for writing this series. And you can trust that if that ever changes, I will disclose it. This is not only required by the FTC here in the US, I strongly believe it is the right thing to do. You can count on me to do so.

## Every Conversation Starts from Scratch

![](https://storage.ghost.io/c/57/9b/579b6dca-f48a-4307-844f-f0533595d058/content/images/2026/04/Claude-in-the-browser.webp)

The challenge with large language models (LLMs) is that by default every conversation starts from scratch. The LLM only knows what you tell it—in that specific conversation.

There are some exceptions. ChatGPT can remember some things about you. Claude can search your past conversations. But for the most part, every conversation wipes the slate clean.

If you, like me, were working on a new landing page, you'd have to upload information about your target customer, the product itself, and the primary and secondary value propositions. You'd have to upload the questions and answers to add to the FAQ. And the testimonials and logos for social proof.

And for my web-based ChatGPT Projects or Claude Project fans, yes you can upload all of this information to a Project and use it across multiple chats. But what happens when you work on the next landing page?

Imagine the next one is for the same target customer but for a different product with a different value proposition. Do you start a new Project? Or do you just add to your existing Project? The former is tedious. The latter muddies the context window (which leads to deteriorating output quality).

This is a real challenge. But I have good news for you. You can solve this problem with Claude Code. You simply have to give it memory.

## What Memory Unlocks

You've already learned in [our Claude Code guide](https://producttalk.org/claude-code-what-it-is-and-how-its-different?ref=producttalk.org) that Claude Code can read the files on your local machine. This is an understated superpower. It means we can use files to create a memory for Claude that will work across all your chat sessions and across any Project.

![](https://storage.ghost.io/c/57/9b/579b6dca-f48a-4307-844f-f0533595d058/content/images/2026/04/Claude-pulls-in-the-files-it-needs-to-do-the-task.webp)When I ask Claude to critique my home page, it fetches the home page, but it also reads my business profile and target audience context files.

Files can be mixed and matched. So you can give Claude exactly what it needs for the task at hand—and nothing more. When you are working on your first landing page, you can reference your target customer and the relevant product. When you are working on your second landing page, you can simply reference the same target customer, but reference the new product.

When you give Claude (or any LLM) the exact right context, you get higher quality output. LLMs benefit from more context, but only if it's relevant to the task at hand.

So to help with your landing page, Claude needs to know about the current product and maybe related products (so it can help differentiate the products), but it doesn't need to know about unrelated products. If we structure our memory files properly, we can give Claude exactly what it needs and nothing more.

When we do this, Claude shifts from being an intern who can tackle one-off tasks to being a trusted advisor and a talented employee who can help you get work done. Claude doesn't have to guess at the value proposition for your product—you've already told it. It already knows how to write in your voice because it has access to your writing guide and plenty of writing samples. It doesn't have to ask who teaches which course. It just knows.

Giving Claude a memory takes a little bit of setup. But it's an investment that compounds over time. You set it up once and can use it forever. You only need to tell it about your target customer once and Claude will always know about it.

If your target customer changes, you simply need to update your text file. Same with your products—you can easily add and remove information as your products evolve. Odds are much of this information already lives in your file system. It's just a matter of making it easy for Claude to use.

And because files live on your local machine, you own them. You can share them across devices. There's no device or vendor lock-in. You decide when and who to share them with. You might work with Claude on one project and ChatGPT (via [Codex](https://openai.com/codex/?ref=producttalk.org)) on another project. Both can rely on the same memory system.

## Design a Three-Layer Memory System

![](https://storage.ghost.io/c/57/9b/579b6dca-f48a-4307-844f-f0533595d058/content/images/2026/04/3-layered-claude-code-memory-strategy.webp)

So how do you actually structure Claude's memory? You need three distinct layers.

Claude Code already encourages you to create two types of context files: global preferences and Project-specific instructions. But there's a third layer that most people miss—and it's where the real power lives.

### Layer 1: Global Preferences (Always on)

![](https://storage.ghost.io/c/57/9b/579b6dca-f48a-4307-844f-f0533595d058/content/images/2026/04/My-Claude.md-file.webp)My global preferences in my ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md file.

The first time you launch Claude Code, it encourages you to create a CLAUDE.md file in your root directory (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md).

This is where you want to store your global preferences. Think about it as your instructions for Claude about how you like to work together, no matter what type of project you are working on.

Mine includes things like:

- Always create a plan for me to review before you start any work
- Give me direct feedback (no hedging, no gentle suggestions)
- Use bullet points for summaries
- Ask clarifying questions one at a time so I can give complete answers
- No emojis unless I explicitly ask for them

Claude Code automatically loads this file at the start of every session, so I never have to explain my preferences again.

### Layer 2: Project-Specific Instructions

![](https://storage.ghost.io/c/57/9b/579b6dca-f48a-4307-844f-f0533595d058/content/images/2026/04/My-Writing-Claude-md-file.webp)This is my Writing/CLAUDE.md file.

Different Projects have different rules. My task management system works differently from my writing workspace, which works differently from my code projects.

For my writing workspace, my Project CLAUDE.md tells Claude:

- I'm the primary writer; Claude is my thought partner and editor
- Multiple review rounds work well: content → structure → accuracy → typos
- Always prioritize human readability over SEO
- Reference the writing style guide when relevant

For my task management system, the project file covers:

- How my [Trello](https://trello.com/?ref=producttalk.org) integration works
- File naming conventions for tasks
- How to process research papers into summaries

For my coding projects, the project file specifies:

- Technology stack ( [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/en?ref=producttalk.org) vs. [Python](https://www.python.org/?ref=producttalk.org))
- Testing framework ( [Jest](https://jestjs.io/?ref=producttalk.org) for Node.js, [pytest](https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/?ref=producttalk.org) for Python)
- Code style and conventions
- Project architecture and directory structure
- Which dependencies and libraries to use

These files live in each project directory as `CLAUDE.md`. When I'm working in that directory, Claude automatically loads those instructions.

### Layer 3: Reference Context (Pull as Needed)—The Real Power

Before we dive into reference context, let's revisit why managing context matters.

LLMs have a context window—a limit to how much information they can process at once. Even when you stay within that limit, research shows that loading too much context degrades performance. There's a phenomenon called "context rot" where the model's output quality deteriorates when the context window gets too full, even if you haven't hit the technical limit.

This means we need to tightly manage what we load into Claude's context. We want small, targeted files—not long, comprehensive ones. Give Claude exactly what it needs for the task at hand, nothing more.

Your CLAUDE.md files get loaded in every relevant session, so keep them concise. You don't want them filling up the context window. Think of them as where you store rules and preferences—how you work, what conventions to follow, what workflows to use.

For more detailed context, create separate context files. You can then reference these as you need them. You can even describe in your CLAUDE.md files what context files exist, and Claude will automatically know to use them.

![](https://storage.ghost.io/c/57/9b/579b6dca-f48a-4307-844f-f0533595d058/content/images/2026/04/Providing-the-Right-Context.webp)When I ask for help creating a landing page, Claude knows to use my business profile, the product file, and my target customers context.

Here's what most people miss: You don't put everything in your global or Project files. Instead, you create separate reference files that Claude only loads when you need them.

Okay, let's get into how I structure my context files. This rest of this article is for paid subscribers. I'll be sharing:

- Exactly which context files I created and why.
- How I got Claude Code to help me create them so that this wasn't a tedious task.
- How I broke them up into small, reusable files so that Claude only gets exactly what it needs for the task at hand.
- How I keep it all up to date.
- Step-by-step instructions for how you can set up a similar memory system.

Let's dive in.

# The Ultimate Guide to Claude's Project Memory: How to Build AI That Actually Knows Your Taste

### The one prompt that builds project memory for you.

[![Wyndo's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!zTXR!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2ac42946-717d-4e50-8477-551c5d7a3025_1638x1638.jpeg)](https://substack.com/@wyndo)

[Wyndo](https://substack.com/@wyndo)

Nov 27, 2025

64

39

6

Share

[![Claude project memory guide showing AI personalization concept](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!0rwf!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4ed9e446-e12d-486f-b255-3c13a0cff65c_1536x1024.png)](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!0rwf!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4ed9e446-e12d-486f-b255-3c13a0cff65c_1536x1024.png)

For the past few months, I’ve been posting 5-7x every week on LinkedIn. Over 250,000 impressions last month with just 2,400 followers.

I know some of you on Substack might cringe. LinkedIn has a reputation - endless humble brags, “grateful to announce” posts, people performing success rather than sharing anything useful.

But here’s my reality: I can’t rely on [Substack](https://aimaker.substack.com/p/how-to-automate-rss-feed-digest-ai-substack-makecom-tutorial) alone to [grow this newsletter](https://aimaker.substack.com/p/how-i-use-ai-system-to-automate-entire-newsletter-vibe-marketing-workflow-grow-newsletter). And honestly? LinkedIn’s been working.

[![LinkedIn analytics showing 250K impressions from AI-written posts](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!kISz!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9f03b18c-8f60-4105-89b8-0028159bdd27_1574x1264.png)](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!kISz!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9f03b18c-8f60-4105-89b8-0028159bdd27_1574x1264.png)

Multiple posts went viral. The math almost feels unfair for such a small account.

The difference came down to one thing: **the hook**.

The right hook does 80% of the work. It stops people mid-scroll, makes them care enough to read, and eventually comment and share. A bad hook? Your best writing dies in silence.

Here’s the thing though - I write my LinkedIn posts with AI. It’s part of my [content repurposing system](https://aimaker.substack.com/p/ai-content-repurposing-automation-system-guide-linkedin-twitter-substack-notes). But getting [Claude](https://aimaker.substack.com/p/the-ultimate-guide-to-turn-claude-project-knowledge-into-your-brain-most-valuable-coworker) to write hooks that actually stop people? That didn’t come easy.

At first, Claude kept generating generic hooks. Safe. Forgettable. The kind that sound fine but don’t make anyone pause.

This happened even though Claude had read my [best-performing posts](https://aimaker.substack.com/p/start-here). Even though it understood my voice. The outputs were fine. But “fine” doesn’t go viral on LinkedIn.

**Until I started using [project memory](https://simonwillison.net/2025/Sep/12/claude-memory/) differently.**

You probably know about AI memory from ChatGPT - how it remembers who you are, picks up on your patterns over time. But ChatGPT’s memory is a black box. You can’t really edit it. You can tweak your system prompt, but you don’t have direct control over what it retains and prioritizes.

Claude Projects work differently. I can edit memory directly. I can tell Claude _exactly_ what to remember, and it carries those instructions into every conversation - not as vague context, but as [decision-making](https://aimaker.substack.com/p/how-ai-literally-shrinking-our-brains-mit-report-meta-learning-socratic-method) criteria.

**So here’s what I did:**

When I noticed Claude wasn’t generating the hooks I wanted, I stopped just fixing the output and moving on. Instead, I asked Claude to adjust using a specific instruction: “Create bold statements that stop readers from scrolling and make them curious to learn more.”

Once I was satisfied with the result, I uploaded the instruction into the project memory **.**

[![How to define your writing taste and customize Claude AI output](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!zFwb!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa4f7bfdd-5a4f-4be2-aa0c-c894e156dff9_2816x1536.png)](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!zFwb!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa4f7bfdd-5a4f-4be2-aa0c-c894e156dff9_2816x1536.png)

For months, I’d been telling AI what I wanted, watching it give me generic outputs, fixing them manually, and repeating the cycle. The same friction kept appearing. The same fixes kept being needed.

Memory changed that. Now the instruction is permanent. Claude doesn’t forget.

But the key to building useful project memory isn’t knowing what to store upfront. It’s paying attention to friction.

The problem is that you can’t notice friction until you know **your own taste**.

That’s the real work. Not configuring AI. Understanding what you actually want - what “good” looks like to you - so clearly that you recognize when AI misses it.

Let me explain.

## What most people do wrong building Claude Projects

Most people start with the [system prompt](https://aimaker.substack.com/p/the-10-step-system-prompt-structure-guide-anthropic-claude) \- which is correct. You document your voice, preferences, audience, tone, background information. Every instruction you can think of.

I wrote a practical, in-depth guide on building Claude’s project knowledge if you want the full method.

[![The Ultimate Guide to Turn Claude Into Your Brain’s Most Valuable Co-Worker](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!SJgx!,w_140,h_140,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep,g_auto/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F3bdcbce6-0f9b-400c-94d7-79928a367474_1536x1024.png)](https://aimaker.substack.com/p/the-ultimate-guide-to-turn-claude-project-knowledge-into-your-brain-most-valuable-coworker)

[**The Ultimate Guide to Turn Claude Into Your Brain’s Most Valuable Co-Worker**](https://aimaker.substack.com/p/the-ultimate-guide-to-turn-claude-project-knowledge-into-your-brain-most-valuable-coworker)

[Wyndo](https://substack.com/profile/556836-wyndo)

·

August 7, 2025

[Read full story](https://aimaker.substack.com/p/the-ultimate-guide-to-turn-claude-project-knowledge-into-your-brain-most-valuable-coworker)

This feels productive. More detail should mean better outputs, right?

And then the outputs still feel off. Not wrong exactly, just not yours.

So you add more. More examples. More clarification. The prompt gets longer, trying to cover every edge case.

I did exactly this.

It helped. But there was still a gap. Outputs that technically followed instructions but didn’t feel like me. Patterns I kept correcting manually.

The problem wasn’t the system [prompt](https://aimaker.substack.com/p/prompt-engineering-deep-dive-guide-simple-steps-10x-ai-results). The problem was I didn’t actually know what I wanted until I saw what I didn’t want.

**That’s where project memory changes everything.**

A system prompt is your starting point - your best guess before you start working together. Project memory is what you learn through the work. The instructions you couldn’t have written upfront because you didn’t know you needed them yet.

The system prompt is your hypothesis. Project memory is your evidence.

And the signal that tells you what to add? Friction.

## Friction-Driven Memory: How to Teach AI Your Preferences

**So what exactly is friction?**

Friction is the gap between what Claude gives you and what you actually needed. That feeling when you read Claude’s output and think:

> _“This is fine, but it’s not right.”_

Most people fix it and move on.

Claude writes a generic hook? Rewrite it manually. Claude sounds too academic? Simplify it yourself. Problem solved. Next task.

But that friction is coming back. Tomorrow. Next week. Every time you work on something similar.

Because Claude doesn’t know it made a mistake. You fixed the output, but you didn’t fix the root cause.

By the third time you’re fixing the same issue, you know it’s a pattern worth addressing. And the corrections you make reveal preferences you couldn’t have articulated upfront - you didn’t know you hated that hook structure until Claude used it.

This only works if you can feel the friction in the first place. You need enough clarity about your preferences to recognize when Claude misses them. But you don’t need perfect taste - you just need to notice when something feels off.

That sensitivity develops through practice. The more you work with Claude and pay attention to what you change, the clearer your preferences become.

## The System: Build Your Claude Project Memory Step by Step

[![Step-by-step friction-driven process to build Claude project memory](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!KB5X!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fff508332-8133-4af7-b57d-1d24426ebc31_2816x1536.png)](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!KB5X!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fff508332-8133-4af7-b57d-1d24426ebc31_2816x1536.png)

So you feel the friction. Now what?

Here’s the process I use to turn friction into permanent project memory.

#### Step 1: Notice friction in real-time (don’t just fix it)

You’re working with Claude. Something in the output bothers you. Your instinct is to fix it quickly and keep moving.

**Stop.**

That moment of friction is valuable. If you fix it and move on, you lose the data.

Instead, pause and ask yourself: _“What specifically is wrong here?”_

**And get specific:**

- Is it the tone? Too formal? Too casual? Too academic?

- Is it the structure? Wrong order? Too long? Missing something?

- Is it a pattern? A phrase structure you hate? A rhetorical device that feels forced?


The more specific you can name the friction, the more useful it becomes.

* * *

#### Step 2: Translate friction into instruction

Here’s where the real work happens.

Friction is a feeling. Project memory needs language. Your job is to translate one into the other.

I use this simple framework:

- **What I got:** \[Claude’s actual output\]

- **What I needed:** \[What would have been right\]

- **The gap:** \[What’s the difference between these two?\]

- **The instruction:** \[What would I tell Claude so this doesn’t happen again?\]


* * *

#### Step 3: Add to Project Memory

Now you take that instruction and make it permanent.

[![Claude project instructions upload interface for custom memory](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!AxX1!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fc1936aab-58f6-45b2-aed5-26103ee275fe_1628x1386.png)](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!AxX1!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fc1936aab-58f6-45b2-aed5-26103ee275fe_1628x1386.png)

In Claude Projects, you can add to memory directly in conversation. Before updating them, I usually say something like:

> _“I noticed the hook you wrote explained the concept before revealing the insight. I need hooks that lead with the surprising claim first. Can you help me write a memory instruction that captures this preference?”_

Claude is actually great at helping you articulate your preferences once you’ve identified the friction.

**A few principles for good memory entries:**

**Be specific, not vague.**

- Bad: “Write better hooks”

- Good: “Lead with the insight first. Don’t set up context before the revelation.”


**Include examples when helpful.**

- Don’t write: ‘Here’s a feature, many people don’t use it, but there’s a pattern...’

- Do write: ‘You don’t know what your project needs until it fails.’


**Name the pattern you’re avoiding.**

- Avoid the ‘They won’t be X, they’ll be Y’ rhetorical contrast pattern.


And make them actionable. Claude should be able to read the instruction and immediately know what to do differently.

* * *

#### Step 4: Test and refine

Memory added. But does it work?

Next time you’re in a similar situation, pay attention. Did Claude follow the instruction? Did the friction disappear?

**If yes:** Great. Move on and wait for the next friction point to surface.

**If no:** The instruction probably wasn’t specific enough. Refine it.

Sometimes I’ll add a memory entry and Claude still doesn’t quite get it. That usually means my instruction was too vague or didn’t capture the real issue. The friction repeating is just more data - it’s telling me to be more precise.

This isn’t a one-and-done process. It’s iterative. Your project memory grows and sharpens over time as you encounter more friction and get better at translating it.

### The Friction-Detection Shortcut

Now that you understand the process, here’s how to speed it up. Sometimes you’re in the middle of a conversation with Claude, making edits and adjustments as you go. You tweak a sentence here. Rewrite a hook there. Ask for a different angle.

You’re in flow. You’re just fixing things without thinking about it.

**But Claude is noticing.**

Claude can see the pattern of your corrections even when you’re too focused to spot it yourself. Every time you adjust something, Claude is tracking what changed and why.

So here’s the trick: ask Claude to tell you what it’s noticing.

During conversation, drop in this prompt:

> _“Looking at our conversation so far, are there any patterns in how I’ve corrected your outputs or asked you to adjust things? What preferences or rules am I demonstrating that aren’t captured in your system prompt or project memory yet?”_

**Why this works:**

- **Claude has perfect recall of the conversation.** You might forget you adjusted tone three times in a row, but Claude doesn’t.

- **It surfaces blind spots.** Friction you didn’t consciously notice gets named and identified.

- **It does the translation work for you.** Instead of manually converting friction into instructions, Claude drafts the memory entries based on observed patterns.


This is the easiest entry point into friction-driven memory. You don’t need to do anything different - just work normally with Claude, then pause and ask what patterns it’s seeing.

### Alternative prompts to try

Depending on what you’re working on, here are variations:

- **After making several corrections:** _“I’ve made several edits to your outputs in this conversation. Can you identify the pattern and suggest a memory instruction that would prevent this friction next time?”_

- **For comprehensive analysis:** _“Based on how I’ve responded to your outputs today - what I kept, what I changed, what I asked you to redo - draft 2-3 specific memory instructions that would improve our collaboration.”_

- **Quick check-in during work:** _“Pause. Have I been correcting the same type of thing repeatedly in the last few chats? If so, what memory instruction would fix it?”_


Use whichever feels most natural to your workflow.

The key is making this a habit: when you notice yourself editing Claude’s outputs multiple times in one session, pause and ask Claude to spot the pattern.

### The cycle in practice

[![Iterative cycle to improve AI output quality with project memory](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!jJlg!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ffc39d742-af0b-4cc9-b7c6-fdb154731bc4_2816x1536.png)](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!jJlg!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ffc39d742-af0b-4cc9-b7c6-fdb154731bc4_2816x1536.png)

Here’s what this looks like day-to-day:

**Option A (Quick):** Work normally → Use friction-detection prompt → Add suggested memory entries

**Option B (Detailed):** Work normally → Notice friction → Translate into instruction → Add to memory → Test

Most of the time, I use Option A. It’s faster and Claude does the pattern-recognition work.

But when I hit friction that’s hard to articulate, I use Option B. The manual translation process forces me to think through exactly what I want - which often reveals preferences I didn’t know I had.

Over time, your project memory becomes a detailed map of your taste - translated into language Claude can actually use.

And here’s the beautiful part: **the friction decreases.**

Not because you’re lowering your standards. Because Claude is genuinely learning what you want. The first draft gets closer to your taste. The edits get smaller. The back-and-forth shrinks.

This is where AI gets better at understanding your taste.

Subscribe

## Claude Project Memory in Practice: Real Examples

Let me show you exactly how this works with real example from one of my projects.

### 1\. The LinkedIn hook problem

**The Situation:**

I’m generating LinkedIn posts. I need hooks that stop people from scrolling - bold, specific, curiosity-driven statements that create immediate tension.

But Claude keeps writing explanatory hooks. Setup before payoff. Context before revelation.

**The Friction:**

I’m writing a post about how enterprises are choosing AI vendors. It’s based on data showing Anthropic’s rapid enterprise market share growth.

Claude gives me:

> _“The AI race is heating up. Companies are making important decisions about which AI providers to use. But the patterns emerging might surprise you.”_

This is backwards. Too much setup. No tension. No specificity. The reader doesn’t feel compelled to keep reading.

I rewrite it manually:

> _“Anthropic just ate 20% of OpenAI’s enterprise market in 24 months. And it’s not even close to done.”_

Bold claim. Specific data. Immediate tension. Now you want to know how this happened.

**The Translation:**

I use the friction-detection prompt mid-conversation:

> _“I’ve adjusted several hooks in this conversation. What pattern are you noticing in my edits?”_

Claude responds:

> _“You consistently move the insight to the front and remove all setup language. You want readers to encounter the surprising claim immediately, then build context after you have their attention. The current system prompt doesn’t explicitly prioritize this structure for hooks.”_

**The Memory Entry:**

```
Hook Framework (LinkedIn):
- Lead with the insight or surprising claim FIRST
- Don’t explain or set up context before the revelation
- Structure: Insight → Context → Explanation
- Make readers think “Wait, what?” before “Oh, I see”
- Avoid: “Here’s X. Most people don’t Y. But there’s Z...”
- Instead: Lead with Z immediately

Example of what NOT to do:
“The AI race is heating up. Companies are making decisions. But patterns might surprise you.”

Example of what TO do:
“Anthropic just ate 20% of OpenAI’s enterprise market in 24 months.”
```

**The Result:**

Next drafts come with compelling hooks instead of explanations. Claude stops easing readers in and starts stopping them mid-scroll.

The hook for my next post about AI workflow adoption came through clean on the first try.

That’s the memory working.

### 2\. The “Tool-Name Creep” Problem

**The Situation:**

I’m generating Substack Notes - short social media posts that need to be universal and relatable to anyone using AI, regardless of which specific tools they’re using.

**My strategy:** focus on transformations and principles, not tool features.

But Claude keeps mentioning specific tool names, which makes insights feel exclusive to users of that particular tool.

**The Friction:**

I brief Claude on my Substack Notes strategy, including the “stay universal” principle.

Claude outputs:

> _“Cursor just changed how I think about coding. Instead of fighting syntax, the AI handles boilerplate and I focus on logic.”_

The insight is solid. But mentioning “Cursor” by name makes it feel like it’s only relevant to Cursor users. My whole strategy depends on keeping insights portable across any AI tool.

I edit it manually:

> _“Programming became collaborative. The AI handles syntax patterns, I focus on problem-solving logic._”

This happens on 4 out of 10 generated notes.

**The Translation:**

I use the friction-detection prompt:

> _“I keep removing tool names from the notes you generate. Can you identify the pattern and suggest a memory instruction?”_

Claude responds:

> _“You’re consistently removing specific tool mentions (Cursor, NotebookLM, Claude) and replacing them with universal descriptions of the transformation. This makes insights work for anyone using AI, not just users of specific tools. The current guidelines mention staying universal, but they don’t explicitly prohibit tool names.”_

**The Memory Entry:**

```
Substack Notes Strategy - Universal Insights:
NEVER mention specific AI tools by name (Cursor, Claude, NotebookLM, ChatGPT, etc.)

Focus on transformations, not tools:
- Instead of “Cursor made me think in systems” → “Thinking in systems changed everything”
- Instead of “Claude taught me to code” → “Programming became collaborative”
- Instead of “NotebookLM synthesized my research” → “Research turned into frameworks”

Your audience is “people using AI” not “people using specific tools.”
Make insights portable. Keep content about universal principles, not tool features.

Test: Would this note still resonate if the reader uses different AI tools? If not, rewrite.
```

**The Result:**

Generated notes are immediately shareable without editing. They work for my entire audience, regardless of which AI tools they’re using.

No more manual culling for tool-name creep. The notes stay focused on the transformation, which is what makes them relatable and shareable.

### 3\. The “Sales email sounds to corporate” problem

**The Situation:**

I’m writing a Black Friday promotional email for my AI Maker Lab premium tier. I need to drive conversions, but I can’t sound like every other marketing email in people’s inboxes.

My voice is optimistic but honest. Direct but not pushy. I want readers to feel conviction, not manipulation.

But Claude keeps defaulting to typical marketing hype.

**The Friction:**

I ask Claude to write urgency for the Black Friday deadline.

**Claude outputs:**

> _“🔥 LAST CHANCE! Don’t miss out on this incredible opportunity! This deal won’t last forever - grab it now before it’s gone! 🚨”_

I physically cringe reading this. It sounds like every spam email I delete without opening. My rule is simple: If I wouldn’t open it, I wouldn’t bother using it.

I rewrite it:

> _“This ends Monday, then we’re back to full price. You’re either subscribing now or waiting another year for this offer.”_

Direct. Honest. Still urgent, but it sounds like me - not like a marketing automation template.

This happens on three different sections of the same email. Claude keeps injecting hype where I want conviction.

**The Translation:**

After the third rewrite, I pause:

> _“I keep removing hype language and emojis from the sales copy. What pattern are you seeing in my edits?”_

Claude responds:

> _“You’re replacing manufactured excitement (’Don’t miss out!’ ‘Incredible opportunity!’) with direct, honest urgency (’This ends Sunday’ ‘You’re either subscribing now or waiting’). You maintain conviction about the value without sounding desperate or salesy. The current instructions don’t distinguish between authentic urgency and typical marketing hype.”_

**The Memory Entry:**

```
VOICE CALIBRATION FOR SALES/PROMOTIONAL CONTENT:
When writing any sales or promotional emails (Black Friday, product launches, conversion campaigns):

MAINTAIN (Authentic conviction):
✅ Direct challenge: “You’re either subscribing now or waiting another year”
✅ Honest urgency: “This ends Sunday, then we’re back to full price”
✅ Confident value statement: “I know Maker Labs delivers real value”
✅ Personal vulnerability: “This only works if you actually implement”
✅ Optimistic but realistic tone: Balance possibility with honesty

AVOID (Typical marketing hype):
❌ Hype energy: “🔥 LAST CHANCE 🔥”
❌ Typical marketing urgency: “Don’t miss out!”
❌ Manufactured excitement that doesn’t match contemplative style
❌ Corporate/salesy language that loses authentic voice
❌ Overselling or exaggeration

Core principle: Keep optimistic voice and conviction WITHOUT sounding salesy.
The line is: authentic urgency and honest value vs. manufactured hype.
```

**The Result:**

Next promotional email comes through without a single emoji or “Don’t miss out!” The urgency lands by being direct instead of desperate.

That’s the difference between AI that mimics marketing templates and AI that understands your actual voice - even when you’re selling.

[Share](https://aimaker.substack.com/p/ultimate-guide-to-claude-project-memory-system-prompt?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&action=share)

## Using Claude’s Knowledge Base for Deeper AI Memory

Once you begin building project memory, you’ll eventually hit a threshold.

The current interface for adding memories limits entries to a few sentences. Some items fit into a single concise paragraph of instruction; others don’t and require more detailed frameworks.

Take my sales voice calibration example from earlier. I could write:

> _“When writing sales emails, avoid corporate hype and maintain authentic voice.”_

But here’s what that instruction doesn’t capture:

- What exactly counts as “corporate hype” vs. “authentic urgency”?

- Where’s the line between conviction and desperation?

- How do I handle limited-time offers without sounding manipulative?


The memory interface lets you write several paragraphs. But trying to document an entire voice calibration framework with examples, edge cases, and decision trees. That’s going to get messy.

**This is where project knowledge base files become essential.**

[![Claude knowledge base files versus project memory instructions](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!_OTy!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fde23ca51-cfa4-4996-9a2c-048e6fa6ae65_2816x1536.png)](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!_OTy!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fde23ca51-cfa4-4996-9a2c-048e6fa6ae65_2816x1536.png)

Think of it this way:

- **Project memory** = Active instructions Claude applies in every conversation

- **Knowledge base files** = Reference frameworks Claude consults when needed


Memory entries should be concise pointers: “Do X, reference Y framework, avoid Z pattern.”

Knowledge base files should be comprehensive documentation: detailed examples, edge cases, before/after comparisons, decision trees, as you can see based on the example section earlier.

**Here’s what you can do:** ask Claude to create the memory for you, generate it as a Markdown file, and upload it to your project knowledge base.

[![Uploading markdown files to Claude project knowledge base](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!qnxG!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F33a3c001-1054-475f-8fef-542ee93d85c0_1330x676.png)](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!qnxG!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F33a3c001-1054-475f-8fef-542ee93d85c0_1330x676.png)

This way, whenever I ask Claude for a sales email, it will mirror my authentic voice and reference the project files for detailed frameworks.

Memory entries force you to distill your taste into clear principles. Knowledge base files let you provide depth without clutter.

The combination is more powerful than trying to cram everything into memory fields or hoping Claude remembers from past conversations.

Concise instructions pointing to detailed references. That’s the system.

To help you improve your taste and understand project memory better, you might want to check out these valuable posts:

1. [The 3-Document System: How I Stopped Losing My Best Ideas Across 100 AI Conversations](https://aimaker.substack.com/p/ai-conversation-management-system-capture-chatgpt-claude-insights-project-memory) by [Ilia Karelin](https://open.substack.com/users/172048615-ilia-karelin?utm_source=mentions)

2. [Reverse-engineer yourself before you train your AI](https://robotsatemyhomework.substack.com/p/reverse-engineer-yourself-before) by [Mia Kiraki 🎭](https://open.substack.com/users/362428399-mia-kiraki?utm_source=mentions)

3. [Why Your Writing Breakthroughs Keep Disappearing](https://aimaker.substack.com/p/why-your-writing-breakthroughs-keep-disappearing-chatgpt-claude-project) by [Greg Wolford](https://open.substack.com/users/4196416-greg-wolford?utm_source=mentions)


## What Training AI Your Writing Style Actually Teaches You

Here’s what I didn’t expect when I started building project memory through friction:

**This process teaches you how your own taste actually works.**

Most people think they know what they want. They have vague preferences: “I like conversational writing” or “I want compelling hooks” or “Keep it simple.”

But those aren’t actionable. They’re directions without coordinates.

Friction forces you to get specific.

When Claude writes something that doesn’t match your taste, you can’t just say “that’s not right” and move on. You have to articulate _why_ it’s not right. You have to name the specific thing that bothers you. You have to translate a feeling into language.

And that’s hard.

It’s hard because most of us haven’t consciously examined our preferences before. We just know what we like when we see it.

But building project memory requires you to reverse-engineer your own taste. To spot patterns in what you keep versus what you change. To notice the invisible rules you’ve been following without realizing they existed.

**This is meta-learning through friction feedback.**

You’re not just teaching Claude. You’re teaching yourself.

## Where to go from here

So here’s where you start:

**This week, open a Claude Project.** If you already have one, pick the one you use most often.

**As you work, pay attention to friction.** When you find yourself editing Claude’s outputs, pause. Don’t just fix it and move on. Notice what you’re changing and why.

**✅ Use the friction-detection prompt.**

Let Claude tell you what it’s noticing. Then take one of those patterns and add it to project memory.

**Start small.** You don’t need to document everything at once. One memory entry this week is enough. One friction point identified and translated into an instruction.

Next week, add another. And another. Over time, your project memory becomes a detailed map of your taste - built through real experience instead of imagination.

* * *

### What you’ll notice

Claude’s outputs will start matching your taste more closely. But the real value is that you’ll understand your own taste more clearly than before. The process of translating friction into instructions sharpens your judgment about your own work.

**You’re not just configuring AI. You’re developing creative self-awareness.**

That’s the unlock.

If you like this post, consider to upgrade and access my **Claude Project Knowledge Guide** instantly!

Subscribe

### The bottom line

Project memory isn’t about storing information. It’s about translating taste into language AI can follow.

And the signal that tells you exactly what to translate? Friction.

You can’t know what your project needs until it fails. But when it fails - when Claude outputs something that doesn’t match your taste - that friction is telling you exactly what instruction to add next.

Don’t ignore it. Don’t just fix it and move on.

**Use it.**

Because friction isn’t a bug in the system. It’s the system.

It’s how taste becomes teachable. It’s how collaboration improves. It’s how you build AI environments that actually work the way you think.

So stop trying to document everything upfront. Stop wondering what you should memorize.

Start working. Notice friction. Translate it into memory. Watch what happens.

That’s how you build project memory that compounds its value over time.

* * *

# How I Finally Sorted My Claude Code Memory \| \#98

### How a single Substack post unlocked Claude Code's hidden memory system - and the full setup prompts to replicate it yourself.

[![John Conneely's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!DBQ7!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F45960688-274f-4086-98f0-c8d8cd48a6c2_1600x1600.jpeg)](https://substack.com/@youngleadersintech)

[John Conneely](https://substack.com/@youngleadersintech)

Mar 18, 2026

59

7

5

Share

[![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!VX5C!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F88cdd555-d996-429b-902b-12a93ccadfa2_1604x900.png)](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!VX5C!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F88cdd555-d996-429b-902b-12a93ccadfa2_1604x900.png)

For weeks - months, if I’m honest - I’d been meaning to sort out Claude’s memory. I’d been posting in Slack channels asking about approaches. In Lenny’s, work, trawling random blog posts. Plenty of people had thoughts. Multiple threads, multiple suggestions, with none shining through as a clear winner.

A lot of what I was seeing was hard to follow - not because the ideas were bad, but because the implementations were opaque. Black boxes sitting in `~/.claude/` doing things I didn’t fully understand. I don’t love that. If I can’t see how something works, I’m reluctant to depend on it. And there was something else nagging at me too: Claude keeps finding its own solutions to these problems.

Overly investing in a custom setup built outside of native Claude functions like `CLAUDE.md` means you might be solving a problem that Claude is already solving - or about to. You could be setting yourself back.

Call it perfectionism dressed up as research.

Either way, I kept not doing it.

There’s a folder at `~/.claude/projects/` that I’d noticed filling up over recent months. Files were appearing in there as I worked. I figured it was something to do with session history, and I hadn’t stopped to find out. I made a mental note a few times to look into it. I didn’t.

* * *

## What Pawel shared

I was on Substack when Pawel Huryn’s post came up. Pawel runs [The Product Compass](https://www.productcompass.pm/) \- if you’re not following him, you should be - and this one had 405 likes and 48 restacks at time of writing. He earned every one of them.

The idea is simple: add an instruction to your `CLAUDE.md` that tells Claude to write useful things down as it learns them, organise them into files, and read them back at the start of each session. Here’s [the post he shared](https://substack.com/@huryn/note/c-216337711?r=1to7jv), and the more advanced structured version of the prompt - which is the one I went with:

```
## Memory Management

Maintain a structured memory system rooted at .claude/memory/

### Structure

- memory.md — index of all memory files, updated whenever you create or modify one
- general.md — cross-project facts, preferences, environment setup
- domain/{topic}.md — domain-specific knowledge (one file per topic)
- tools/{tool}.md — tool configs, CLI patterns, workarounds

### Rules

1. When you learn something worth remembering, write it to the right file immediately
2. Keep memory.md as a current index with one-line descriptions
3. Entries: date, what, why — nothing more
4. Read memory.md at session start. Load other files only when relevant
5. If a file doesn't exist yet, create it

### Maintenance

When I say "reorganize memory":
1. Read all memory files
2. Remove duplicates and outdated entries
3. Merge entries that belong together
4. Split files that cover too many topics
5. Re-sort entries by date within each file
6. Update memory.md index
7. Show me a summary of what changed
```

What made this one stick when other approaches hadn’t was the format. It wasn’t a thread to read. It wasn’t an architecture to design before you could start. It was a prompt. Drop it in, done.

Within a few minutes of dropping in this version - the one with folders for `tools/`, `domain/`, and `general.md` \- something happened that I wasn’t expecting at all.

Claude looked at those files I’d noticed accumulating in `~/.claude/projects/`. And it reorganised them.

> Side note: Auto memory shipped in v2.1.59 on 26 February 2026 - about a week before I wrote this. If you’re on a personal Pro or Max plan you’re likely already on it. If you’re on an enterprise plan, your organisation may have auto-updates disabled - run `claude doctor` in your terminal to check your version and update status.
>
> It’s scoped per project - one memory directory per git repository, or per working directory if you’re outside a git repo. The folder names in `~/.claude/projects/` are just your paths with slashes replaced by hyphens, so `/Users/john/projects/my-app` becomes `-Users-john-projects-my-app`.
>
> Sort by recently modified - anything touched after 26 February should have a `memory/` folder inside if auto-update was on.
>
> If you haven’t looked yet, go check. Claude may already have been quietly keeping notes on your work - something I didn’t know was happening myself.

The files I’d assumed were session history turned out to be more than that. Tucked in alongside them was a `memory/` folder - Claude had already started building notes on my projects in the week since the feature shipped, and once I gave it a framework to work with, it had something real to apply it to. A week of quietly accumulated context, already there waiting for someone to organise it.

* * *

## Which is when I saw the real problem

Having it working immediately surfaced something I’d been half-aware of for a while.

I have a lot going on in my Claude Code setup - agents, skills, custom commands, hooks, integrations with Jira, Snowflake, Confluence, Slack - months of configuration, all built up gradually.

Every time I solved something in one part of the setup - the right way to authenticate with Confluence, an exact format for a Jira custom field, a Snowflake schema detail I have to work around - I ended up needing to apply that same fix in twenty other places too. More often than not I didn’t actually implement the change everywhere, and only fixed things as I encountered them, which meant the solution drifted slightly each time. Doing things in dribs and drabs like this, with the speed of updates to Claude, leaves a bunch of different functionality or tools trying to solve for the same problem.

A flat `memory.md` wouldn’t solve this cleanly either - it would just be the same large `CLAUDE.md` file pattern most of you have already experienced. It gets appended to every session and eventually becomes a sprawling document that Claude loads whether it needs half of the context or not. The right knowledge loading at the wrong time, or not loading at all because it’s buried.

More memory is not the answer. Better architecture is.

The structured version - the one with subdirectories for different categories of knowledge - solves this properly.

* * *

## The structure I landed on

```
~/.claude/memory/
  memory.md           <- index (read at session start)
  general.md          <- cross-project conventions
  tools/
    snowflake.md
    atlassian.md
    slack.md
  domain/
    {product}/
      {product}.md
    {project}/
      {project}.md
```

`memory.md` is the index - one line per topic file, read every session.

`general.md` holds things that apply everywhere: naming conventions, and workflow preferences. I’ve since pulled writing preferences into its own file too - once you start publishing regularly, it earns its own space.

`tools/` is where tool-specific knowledge lives - the exact way I’ve configured each integration, the quirks I’ve found, what works and what doesn’t in my specific setup. How Confluence authentication actually behaves. What the Atlassian CLI can and can’t do. Things that would take ten minutes to explain from scratch every time.

`domain/` is where things get more interesting.

This is the most important part of the structure, and the one with the most room to grow. When you start working in a new domain - a product area, a codebase, a feature, a business process - that knowledge goes into `domain/{topic}/`. It builds up over time as you work: the gotchas, the edge cases, the context that takes you weeks to accumulate.

But the real potential here is sharing. If you can sync domain files with teammates, subject matter experts can contribute their hard-won context directly. That knowledge becomes a living file - not documentation nobody reads, but the specific, granular detail that Claude actually needs to be useful in that domain. The boundaries of a domain only get tighter the more specific you get.

At some point there’s enough to package as a skill - a reusable plugin that can be invoked across any project. For a product I work on, the domain knowledge is now a full skill, and it gets updated constantly. When the memory file gets too large for itself, I raise a pull request to either promote it into a skill or add it as an addendum to one that already exists.

The memory entry itself becomes a pointer. Three lines pointing at the skill. The knowledge lives in the skill. The memory file just says where to find it.

### Global vs project memory

Global memory at `~/.claude/memory/` holds knowledge that applies across everything - tools, conventions, credential patterns. Project memory at `~/.claude/projects/{project}/memory/MEMORY.md` holds knowledge specific to one repo - the active tickets, the doc structure, patterns specific to that codebase.

I have a standing instruction in my `CLAUDE.md` to check whether `MEMORY.md` exists at the start of any session in a new project - and if it doesn’t, create the standard structure. The first time Claude opens a new project, it sets up the scaffolding. After that, it fills it in as it goes. The hook in Part 2 acts as the backup - it injects memory automatically before every tool call, so even if the instruction gets missed, the context is still there.

* * *

## The bit I got wrong first

My first version put the trigger table - the routing rules that tell Claude which topic files to load for which context - inside the project `MEMORY.md`. That’s the file auto memory loads at session start, so it seemed like the right place. If I wanted something read automatically, that’s where it should live. Made sense at the time.

It’s exactly wrong.

The project `MEMORY.md` only loads the first 200 lines at session start. That’s not a preference - it’s how Anthropic have built the feature. Burn 20 of those lines on routing rules and you’ve given up 10% of your budget on boilerplate that could live somewhere else. Specifically: in `CLAUDE.md`, which loads in full. Routing rules are instructions, not learnings. They belong in `CLAUDE.md`, not in the auto memory file.

While sorting that out, the question of reliable loading came up. Claude Code hooks are scripts that fire automatically at specific points in a session - before a tool runs, after a response, at session start, etc. I wanted something that would inject my project memory automatically, without relying on Claude remembering to read it. A backup for when the instruction gets missed.

The hook needed to fire once per session context - including for new subagents, which each get their own process.

> One small hook technical detail worth flagging: `CLAUDE_SESSION_ID` doesn’t exist in hooks - I learned that the hard way. The right identifier is `os.getppid()`. I’ll cover the full hook setup in the Try it yourself section below.

Twenty minutes later the structure was clean.

* * *

Once the structure was in place, I switched into plan mode and ran “reorganize memory”. Plan mode matters here - you’re potentially changing a lot of files, and you want to see what Claude is going to do before it does it. Nothing untoward ending up in important places.

`~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` went from 189 lines to 63 lines. Seven new files in `~/.claude/memory/` covering tools and domain knowledge that had previously been crammed inline. Three new files in project memory for the repo I’m currently working in. A pointer entry for a product I work on. An auto-init instruction so every new project gets the structure from day one.

One thing worth doing while you’re at it: create a git repo for your `~/.claude/` folder. If you’re keeping it private, you can commit everything. If you’re sharing it, ask Claude what to gitignore - it’ll know what’s sensitive and what isn’t.

The bloated `CLAUDE.md` that had been loading 155 lines of dense reference content on every single session is now 63 lines pointing at files that load when they’re needed.

Small thing. Compounds.

* * *

## Try it yourself

You can start with Pawel’s basic version above if you like. It’s a nice entry point - it works in about ten minutes and the improvement is immediate. You don’t need the folder structure to get value from it.

I personally prefer mine, but it is a big longer and more involved from a prompt aspect. The time taken is still the same, but Pawel’s is enough for everyone and that’s what he is going for and he excels with his blog. I love tweaking stuff for UX reasons, and this is one of those.

If you’re in the position I was in - lots of tools, context that needs to live in more than one place, knowledge that keeps needing to be re-entered - the structured version is worth the extra setup. Pawel’s prompt got me started, but what’s below is what I actually have in my `CLAUDE.md` now, after a few days of refinement.

Both prompts run in plan mode - Claude shows you what it’s going to do before it touches anything, and nothing gets overwritten without asking.

* * *

### Part 1: Memory structure

Start here. Sets up the memory architecture and teaches Claude Code how to use it. No hooks, no changes to `settings.json`.

* * *

_**\-\-\- Copy this whole markdown section for Part 1 ---**_

````
Set up a structured, persistent memory management system for Claude Code. Run in plan mode, show me the plan, then execute.

## Before starting

Create tasks for each step below using TaskCreate, then mark each in_progress before starting it and completed when done:

1. Create global memory directory structure
2. Update CLAUDE.md
3. Initialise project MEMORY.md files

**Rule: if any file already exists and would be modified or removed, use AskUserQuestion first. Show the current content and the proposed change. Do not modify without explicit confirmation.**

---

## 1. Global memory directory structure

Create `~/.claude/memory/` with the following files if they do not already exist:

`~/.claude/memory/memory.md` — the index file:

```
# Memory Index

Read this file at session start. Load specific topic files only when relevant.

| File | Description | Last updated |
|------|-------------|--------------|
| `general.md` | Cross-project conventions and preferences | {today} |

## Cross-Memory Sync Rule

At session start, after reading this file:
1. Note the Last updated dates in the table above
2. Check projects.md (if it exists) for active project MEMORY.md paths
3. If any project MEMORY.md has content worth promoting to a global tools/ or domain/ file, flag it
4. Update the Last updated date on this file after any changes

## Domain Knowledge Lifecycle

1. Staging — knowledge accumulates in domain/{name}/
2. Promotion — enough knowledge exists to package as a plugin/skill
3. Pointer — after promotion, the memory file becomes a pointer to the plugin
```

`~/.claude/memory/general.md` — the cross-project conventions file:

```
# General - Cross-Project Conventions

## Writing & Naming Conventions

(Populate as you go — add things like: preferred date formats, naming patterns, style preferences)

## Workflow Preferences

(Populate as you go — add things like: how you prefer to review work, commit message style)
```

Also create empty directories `~/.claude/memory/tools/` and `~/.claude/memory/domain/` if they do not exist (these fill up over time as you work).

---

## 2. Update CLAUDE.md

If `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` does not exist, create it as an empty file first.

For each of the sections below, check if the section header already exists in the file. If it does, leave it alone. If it does not, add it:
- New or empty file: add sections at the top
- File with existing content: add sections after the last existing section

**Section: Memory Management**

```
## Memory Management

Maintain a structured memory system rooted at .claude/memory/

### Structure

- memory.md — index of all memory files, updated whenever you create or modify one
- general.md — cross-project facts, preferences, environment setup
- domain/{topic}.md — domain-specific knowledge (one file per topic)
- tools/{tool}.md — tool configs, CLI patterns, workarounds

### Rules

1. When you learn something worth remembering, write it to the right file immediately
2. Keep memory.md as a current index with one-line descriptions
3. Entries: date, what, why — nothing more
4. Read memory.md at session start. Load other files only when relevant
5. If a file doesn't exist yet, create it
6. Before removing or modifying any existing memory entry, use AskUserQuestion to confirm
with the user — show the current content and the proposed change

### Maintenance

When I say "reorganize memory":
1. Read all memory files
2. Remove duplicates and outdated entries
3. Merge entries that belong together
4. Split files that cover too many topics
5. Re-sort entries by date within each file
6. Update memory.md index
7. Show me a summary of what changed
```

**Section: Global Memory**

```
## Global Memory

Read ~/.claude/memory/memory.md at session start. Load specific topic files only when relevant.

Topic files:
- ~/.claude/memory/general.md — cross-project conventions and preferences
```

**Section: Global Memory Reference Rule**

```
## Global Memory Reference Rule

Whenever you work in a project and read (or create) its MEMORY.md, check that it contains a
## Global Memory section. If it does not, add it near the top, after the H1.

The section must be a SHORT POINTER only. Do NOT duplicate the topic file list into project
MEMORY.md. The list lives in CLAUDE.md (single source of truth). Project MEMORY.md has a
200-line budget — use it for project knowledge, not boilerplate.

Canonical template for project MEMORY.md:

## Global Memory

Read ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md for memory rules and topic files.

When a new file is added to ~/.claude/memory/:
- Add it to the ## Global Memory topic file list in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md only
- Do NOT update individual project MEMORY.md files
```

**Section: Repo Memory Auto-Init**

```
## Repo Memory Auto-Init

At session start in any project, check for MEMORY.md in the project memory directory
(~/.claude/projects/{mapped-path}/memory/). If it does not exist, create it:

# {Project Name} - Project Memory

## Global Memory

Read ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md for memory rules and topic files.

## Project Notes

(Populated as you work in this project)
```

**Section: Domain Knowledge Lifecycle**

```
## Domain Knowledge Lifecycle

1. Staging — knowledge accumulates in ~/.claude/memory/domain/{name}/
2. Promotion — enough knowledge exists to package as a plugin/skill
3. Pointer — after promotion, the memory file becomes a pointer to the plugin;
content lives in the plugin

When an update is needed to a promoted domain, note it in the memory file so an issue
can be created on the plugin repo.
```

---

## 3. Initialise project MEMORY.md files

Scan `~/.claude/projects/` for existing project directories. For each one:

a) If `memory/MEMORY.md` does not exist: create it with the stub template above (use the directory name as the project name, formatted readably).

b) If `memory/MEMORY.md` exists and contains a ## Global Memory section with a trigger table (a markdown table with Trigger/Load columns): replace that section with the 2-line pointer. Do not modify any other content in the file.

c) If `memory/MEMORY.md` exists with a correct pointer already: leave it alone.
````

* * *

_End of Part 1. Once Claude has built the plan for Part 1, switch into plan mode again and run “ **reorganize memory**” to tidy up what Claude Code has already built._

* * *

> A note on Atlassian MCP: you’ll notice it’s not in my tools list. I banned it. It’s unreliable, it absolutely gobbles tokens, and it caused a corrupt data entry in one of my registries that poisoned a bunch of output files before I caught it.
>
> I’ve migrated to ACLI and direct REST API for Jira, and direct REST API for Confluence - both are in my tools files. The painful part: most of my agents were built assuming the MCP. I haven’t migrated them yet. That’s a job for another weekend.

* * *

### **Part 2: PreToolUse hook (optional)**

Run this after Part 1. This adds a hook that injects your project memory and global index before the first tool call of every session - including for new subagents. It survives context compression in long sessions. Two-file design: a bash wrapper (~5ms) gates a Python script so the overhead after the first call is negligible.

Dependency: `python3` must be on your PATH. Run `which python3` in your terminal to confirm.

_**\-\-\- Copy this whole markdown section for Part 2 ---**_

````
Add automatic memory injection to Claude Code. Run in plan mode, show me the plan, then execute.

## Before starting

Create tasks for each step below using TaskCreate, then mark each in_progress before starting it and completed when done:

1. Create PreToolUse memory hook files
2. Register hook in settings.json
3. Update ## Global Memory section in CLAUDE.md to reference the hook

**Rule: if any file already exists and would be modified or removed, use AskUserQuestion first. Show the current content and the proposed change. Do not modify without explicit confirmation.**

---

## 1. PreToolUse memory hook files

Create two files in `~/.claude/hooks/` (create the directory if it doesn't exist). **Create the `.py` file first, then the `.sh` file** — the wrapper calls the Python script, so it needs to exist before the hook is active.

`~/.claude/hooks/pre-tool-memory.py` — the memory injector (create this first):

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""PreToolUse hook: inject project MEMORY.md on first tool call of this process context."""
import json
import os
import sys
from pathlib import Path

def main():
    # Use parent process ID as session identifier
    # PPID = Claude Code process — stable within a session, new for each subagent
    ppid = os.getppid()
    flag_path = Path(f"/tmp/claude-memory-loaded-{ppid}")

    # Already loaded for this process — exit silently (no output = no context injection)
    if flag_path.exists():
        sys.exit(0)

    # Mark as loaded for this process
    flag_path.touch()

    project_dir = os.environ.get('CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR', os.getcwd())

    # Map project dir to .claude/projects key
    # /Users/you/Projects/foo -> -Users-you-Projects-foo
    # Replace / and . with -, keep the leading - (don't lstrip)
    mapped = project_dir.replace('/', '-').replace('.', '-')

    home = Path.home()
    memory_file = home / '.claude' / 'projects' / mapped / 'memory' / 'MEMORY.md'
    global_idx = home / '.claude' / 'memory' / 'memory.md'

    parts = []

    if memory_file.exists():
        lines = memory_file.read_text().splitlines()[:200]
        parts.append(f"=== Project Memory: {project_dir} ===\n" + '\n'.join(lines))
    else:
        parts.append(f"(no project MEMORY.md at {memory_file})")

    if global_idx.exists():
        parts.append("=== Global Memory Index ===\n" + global_idx.read_text())

    context = '\n\n'.join(parts)

    output = {
        "hookSpecificOutput": {
            "hookEventName": "PreToolUse",
            "additionalContext": context
        }
    }

    print(json.dumps(output))
    sys.exit(0)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

`~/.claude/hooks/pre-tool-memory.sh` — the shell wrapper (create this second):

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Shell wrapper for pre-tool-memory.py
# Checks the PPID flag before invoking Python — ~5ms overhead vs ~80ms for Python startup
FLAG="/tmp/claude-memory-loaded-$(ps -o ppid= -p $$ | tr -d ' ')"
[ -f "$FLAG" ] && exit 0
[ -f ~/.claude/hooks/pre-tool-memory.py ] || exit 0
exec python3 ~/.claude/hooks/pre-tool-memory.py
```

Make both files executable:

```bash
chmod +x ~/.claude/hooks/pre-tool-memory.sh ~/.claude/hooks/pre-tool-memory.py
```

---

## 2. Register the hook in settings.json

Read `~/.claude/settings.json`. If a `PreToolUse` key already exists inside `hooks`, add a new entry to it. If it does not exist, add the following block inside the `hooks` object (create `hooks` if it doesn't exist):

```json
"PreToolUse": [\
{\
    "matcher": "*",\
    "hooks": [\
      {\
        "type": "command",\
        "command": "bash ~/.claude/hooks/pre-tool-memory.sh",\
        "timeout": 5\
      }\
    ]\
}\
]
```

---

## 3. Update ## Global Memory in CLAUDE.md

Read `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`. Find the `## Global Memory` section. Replace its description line with the following (leave the topic file list unchanged):

Replace this line:
```
Read ~/.claude/memory/memory.md at session start. Load specific topic files only when relevant.
```

With:
```
Project MEMORY.md and this index are auto-injected before each tool call via PreToolUse hook
(~/.claude/hooks/pre-tool-memory.sh). Load specific topic files only when relevant.
```
````

_End of Part 2. If you’ve already run either prompt before, it’s safe to run again - Claude checks before creating or modifying anything, and won’t overwrite without asking._

* * *

### **What I ended up with in 5 days**

[![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!2zOO!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff296e242-baca-4fa8-888b-24e8a3f69591_807x846.png)](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!2zOO!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff296e242-baca-4fa8-888b-24e8a3f69591_807x846.png)

Can you do it?

Of course. Before you run anything, check what Claude Code has already built in `~/.claude/projects/` \- you might be surprised by what’s already there in the memory folders that exist.

Then run Part 1, switch into plan mode and run “reorganize memory”, and add Part 2 when you’re ready for the hook.

Pawel recently posted a follow-up covering error logging and knowledge graduation - I’ve incorporated that into my own setup too above, so thanks Pawel. [Worth a read](https://substack.com/@huryn/note/c-228204100?r=1to7jv) if the domain lifecycle section above resonated. That maps to a different problem I’ve been sitting on since January. More on that soon.

## Description

[![@arsmap](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32430831?u=4fe824098595073a90a0a672e83a92967291299b&v=4&size=48)](https://github.com/arsmap)

[arsmap](https://github.com/arsmap)

opened [on Feb 15on Feb 15, 2026](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/25947#issue-3944591054)

Issue body actions

## Current Behavior

Project-specific memory files are stored in a global path:

```
~/.claude/projects/<encoded-project-path>/memory/MEMORY.md
```

## Desired Behavior

Store memory files inside the project's own `.claude/memory/` folder, e.g.:

```
<project-root>/.claude/memory/MEMORY.md
```

This is more intuitive — when working on multiple projects with separate sessions, users naturally expect each project's memory to live within that project's directory, not in a centralized global location.

## Concern: Accidental Git Commits

Memory files contain personal session history and should **never** be committed to version control. To address this:

- If memory files are created in a project-local `.claude/` folder (whether by user request or via a future feature/option), Claude Code should **automatically ensure**`.claude/memory/` is listed in `.gitignore` **before any git commit**.
- If `.gitignore` doesn't exist, create it with the entry.
- If `.gitignore` exists but doesn't include the path, append it.

This would make the feature safe by default while keeping the project structure clean and self-contained.

## Additional Context

The project-level `.claude/` folder already exists for shared settings like `CLAUDE.md`. Adding a `memory/` subfolder there (with proper `.gitignore` protection) would be a natural extension of the existing structure.

More actions

I support this. The way currently memories are stored is fragile - it would break if any of the directories along the path to the project on users' disk is renamed or the project directory is moved. And it indeed makes it difficult to share across machines and share with team. This is simply a bad design.

Temporary workaround: I did put instructions in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md to override this (and store those memories in /CLAUDE\_NOTES.md.

More actions

## Idea: Allow relative paths in autoMemoryDirectory from project settings

I've been running into the same fragmentation issues described here and in related issues ( [#25947](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/25947), [#24382](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/24382), [#25739](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/25739), [#24789](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/24789), [#30668](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/30668)).

While exploring workarounds, I realized that many of these issues might be addressed by a relatively small change: allowing `autoMemoryDirectory` to accept relative paths from project-level `.claude/settings.json`.

### Example: Per-project isolated memory

```
// .claude/settings.json (project level)
{ "autoMemoryDirectory": "./.claude/memory" }
```

### Example: Shared memory across worktrees

```
// In each worktree's .claude/settings.json
{ "autoMemoryDirectory": "../sharedMemory" }
```

```
project/                          ← main repo
├── sharedMemory/MEMORY.md        ← shared across all worktrees
├── worktree-feature-a/
│   └── .claude/settings.json     → "../sharedMemory"
├── worktree-feature-b/
│   └── .claude/settings.json     → "../sharedMemory"
```

This would let users decide their own memory structure — isolated per project, shared across worktrees, or synced across machines via Git.

### Current blockers (as far as I can tell)

1. `autoMemoryDirectory` is blocked from project-level settings for security reasons
2. Relative paths seem to silently fall back to the default location

### A possible compromise

To address the security concern, perhaps relative paths could be restricted to stay within the project tree (e.g., allow `./.claude/memory` or `../sharedMemory` but reject paths that escape the project root).

Just an idea — would love to hear if others think this approach could work or if there are concerns I'm missing.
